1: idete potapen agapen — see what kind of love — potapos (what kind, what country of origin) is a word of astonishment at the foreign, exotic quality of this love; it is not ordinary human love scaled up but something categorically from another source. techna theou klethenomen — called children of God — the verb kaleo (to call) carries the sense of naming into reality; God's calling makes the adoption real. kai esmen — and we are — the parenthetical reassurance that the name is not mere title but ontological reality.
2: homoioi auto esometha — we will be like him — the most concentrated theosis-language in the NT epistles. Homoioi does not mean numerically identical but sharing in the likeness of his glory. hoti opsometha auton kathos estin — because we will see him as he is — the causal structure is remarkable: vision produces transformation. The beatific vision is not the reward for prior perfection but the means by which the transformation occurs. This inverts platonic hierarchy (ethics first, then vision) by making the eschatological vision itself the transformative event.
4: hamartia estin he anomia — sin is lawlessness (literally: sin is the lawlessness) — the article makes this a definitional equation: whatever sin is, it is by nature anomia (not-law, anti-law, lawlessness). This is not merely breaking a rule but a posture of anti-covenantal defiance. The definition excludes any category of sin as merely accidental or excusable social error; all sin stands in the same relation to God's law as deliberate covenant-breaking.
8: lyse (luo) — destroy, loose, undo — the verb used for dissolving bonds, releasing captives, and dismantling structures. The Son of God appeared (ephanerothe) for this purpose: the unraveling of the devil's entire project. ta erga tou diabolou — the works of the devil — not merely individual sins but the system-of-works that the devil has constructed: deception, accusation, death, bondage. The same verb appears in Eph 2:14 (Christ loosed the dividing wall) and in eschatological contexts of dissolution.
14: metabebakamen ek tou thanatou eis ten zoen — we have passed from death to life (perfect tense, completed transition still in effect) — the perfect tense indicates a decisive past event with permanent present result. The criterion given is hoti agapomen tous adelphous — because we love the brothers — love is not the cause of the passage but the evidence and test of whether the passage has occurred. The one who does not love (ho me agapon) remains (menei) in death; love and life are inseparable Johannine realities.
3: ἁγνίζει ἑαυτόν — "purifies himself." Ἁγνίζω (G48) — to make pure; related to ἅγιος (holy) but with emphasis on the moral purity that distinguishes what is consecrated. The active voice and reflexive ἑαυτόν: the believer participates in their own purification — the hope motivates a present ethical response. καθὼς ἐκεῖνος ἁγνός ἐστιν — "just as that one is pure." Ἐκεῖνος — John's elevated demonstrative for Christ (see 2:6). Present-tense ἐστίν: Christ's purity is his ongoing state, the standard being aimed at.
5: ἐφανερώθη — "was manifested" (aorist passive). The appearance of Christ serves a specific purpose: ἵνα τὰς ἁμαρτίας ἄρῃ — "to take away sins." Αἴρω (G142, aorist subjunctive here) = to lift, carry away — not merely to cover or forgive but to remove. The same verb appears in John 1:29 ("Behold, the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world"). ἁμαρτία ἐν αὐτῷ οὐκ ἔστιν — "in him there is no sin" (anarthrous, emphatic). His sinlessness is the ground for his sin-removing work.
6:
πᾶς ὁ ἐν αὐτῷ μένων οὐχ ἁμαρτάνει — "everyone who abides in him does not sin." Both μένων (abiding) and ἁμαρτάνει (sins) are present participle and present indicative — ongoing states. The claim is about habitual pattern, not sinless perfection (cf. 1:8-10). The one whose characteristic mode of existence is abiding does not have sin as their characteristic mode of existence.

πᾶς ὁ ἁμαρτάνων οὐχ ἑώρακεν αὐτὸν οὐδὲ ἔγνωκεν αὐτόν — "everyone who sins has not seen him nor known him." Perfect tenses: the absence of genuine past experience of Christ is evidenced in the present pattern of sin.
7: μηδεὶς πλανάτω ὑμᾶς — "let no one lead you astray." Present imperative of πλανάω — the same verb as 2:26 (πλανώντων). The danger is ongoing. ὁ ποιῶν τὴν δικαιοσύνην δίκαιός ἐστιν — "the one who does righteousness is righteous." Greek identifies the person by their practice: there is no righteousness status that exists apart from righteous practice. Δικαιοσύνη (G1343, dispute_level 4) — here in its behavioral sense (right-living), though the forensic root (righteous standing before God) underlies it.
9:
πᾶς ὁ γεγεννημένος ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ ἁμαρτίαν οὐ ποιεῖ — "everyone who has been born of God does not practice sin." Γεγεννημένος: perfect passive participle — "the one who has been born" — the birth is a completed, ongoing state. The sinning (ποιεῖ, present) that is denied is habitual practice.

ὅτι σπέρμα αὐτοῦ ἐν αὐτῷ μένει — "because his seed remains in him." Σπέρμα (G4690): the "seed" of God. Three main interpretations exist: (1) the divine nature itself; (2) the Holy Spirit as agent of new birth; (3) the word of God as instrument of regeneration (cf. James 1:18; 1 Pet 1:23). All three find support in Johannine theology; the term may be deliberately polyvalent. The point: the divine generative principle abides (μένει, present) in the born-of-God person, making sin-as-lifestyle impossible.
10: ἐν τούτῳ φανερά ἐστιν τὰ τέκνα τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ τὰ τέκνα τοῦ διαβόλου — "in this the children of God and the children of the devil are manifest." Φανερός (evident, visible) uses the phan- root running through the letter. The two families become visible by their characteristic practices. The criteria are twofold: δικαιοσύνη (righteousness) and ἀγάπη τοῦ ἀδελφοῦ (love of the brother). Neither alone suffices; together they constitute the distinguishing mark.
11: ἀγγελία (angelia, G31) — "message," appearing only here and at 1:5 in the NT. The letter frames its two great declarations as messages received and transmitted: "God is light" (1:5) and "we should love one another" (3:11). Both are sourced in apostolic proclamation from the beginning — not new revelations but original announcements. ἀπ᾽ ἀρχῆς — "from the beginning" — the same phrase as 1:1, 2:7,13,14,24.
12: Κάιν — Cain appears only here in the NT epistles as a type (Heb 11:4 mentions Abel). ἐκ τοῦ πονηροῦ ἦν — "was of the evil one" (ἐκ, origin/allegiance). ἔσφαξεν (G4969) — "slew/slaughtered." The verb is used in Revelation for the slaughter of the Lamb — a pointed contrast with the lamb whose blood cleanses. χάριν τίνος ἔσφαξεν αὐτόν — "for what reason did he slaughter him?" John answers: ὅτι τὰ ἔργα αὐτοῦ πονηρὰ ἦν τὰ δὲ τοῦ ἀδελφοῦ αὐτοῦ δίκαια — "because his works were evil and his brother's were righteous." Righteousness provokes murderous hatred in those whose origin is the evil one.
13: μὴ θαυμάζετε — "do not be surprised/marvel" (present imperative with negation). World hatred of believers is not anomalous but structurally predictable: Cain established the pattern. εἰ μισεῖ ὑμᾶς ὁ κόσμος — "if the world hates you." The εἰ + indicative is a first-class condition — the reality of the world's hatred is assumed, not hypothetical.
15: πᾶς ὁ μισῶν τὸν ἀδελφὸν αὐτοῦ ἀνθρωποκτόνος ἐστίν — "everyone who hates his brother is a murderer." The move from Cain's external act to the internal disposition reflects Jesus' logic in Matt 5:21-22, now formalized as a categorical identification. Ἀνθρωποκτόνος (G443) = man-killer; the same word used in John 8:44 for the devil. οὐδεὶς ἀνθρωποκτόνος ἔχει ζωὴν αἰώνιον ἐν αὐτῷ μένουσαν — "no murderer has eternal life remaining in him." Μένουσαν — present participle: the life does not have residence there.
16:
ἐν τούτῳ ἐγνώκαμεν τὴν ἀγάπην — "by this we have come to know love." Ἐγνώκαμεν: perfect of γινώσκω — we have come to know and continue to know. The object is not a concept but a reality disclosed by a specific historical act. ὅτι ἐκεῖνος ὑπὲρ ἡμῶν τὴν ψυχὴν αὐτοῦ ἔθηκεν — "because that one laid down his life for us." Ἐκεῖνος — John's elevated demonstrative for Christ. Τίθημι τὴν ψυχήν = "to lay down the soul/life" — the identical phrase as John 10:11,15,17,18. The act was voluntary and unrepeated (aorist ἔθηκεν).

καὶ ἡμεῖς ὀφείλομεν ὑπὲρ τῶν ἀδελφῶν τὰς ψυχὰς θεῖναι — "and we are obligated to lay down our lives for the brothers and sisters." Ὀφείλομεν — moral obligation (G3784), same as 2:6. The cross is not only the ground of forgiveness but the pattern for community life.
17: ὃς δ᾽ ἂν ἔχῃ τὸν βίον τοῦ κόσμου — "whoever has the world's possessions." Βίος (G979) — material means of living, distinct from ζωή (spiritual life). θεωρῇ τὸν ἀδελφὸν αὐτοῦ χρείαν ἔχοντα — "and sees his brother having need." Θεωρέω = to behold carefully, observe attentively — a present participle of watching, not mere noticing. κλείσῃ τὰ σπλάγχνα αὐτοῦ ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ — "shuts his compassion from him." Σπλάγχνα (G4698): entrails — the Greek seat of deep emotion and compassion (as English uses "gut" or "heart"). To shut the splancha is to clamp down on the movement of compassion. πῶς ἡ ἀγάπη τοῦ θεοῦ μένει ἐν αὐτῷ — "how does God's love remain in him?" The question is rhetorical: it cannot.
18: μὴ ἀγαπῶμεν λόγῳ μηδὲ τῇ γλώσσῃ — "let us not love in word nor in tongue." Two instruments of verbal expression — λόγος (the utterance, the content) and γλῶσσα (the tongue, the organ). ἀλλὰ ἐν ἔργῳ καὶ ἀληθείᾳ — "but in deed and in truth." The contrast is not between speech and silence but between empty declaration and enacted reality. In Johannine usage, truth is not merely propositional but a reality that must be lived (ποιεῖν τὴν ἀλήθειαν, "doing the truth," 1:6).
19: ἐν τούτῳ γνωσόμεθα ὅτι ἐκ τῆς ἀληθείας ἐσμέν — "by this we will know that we are of the truth." Γνωσόμεθα (future of γινώσκω) — assurance comes from observing love-in-deed, not from introspection alone. Ἐκ τῆς ἀληθείας — origin again: to be "of the truth" means to have truth as one's source. καὶ ἔμπροσθεν αὐτοῦ πείσομεν τὰς καρδίας ἡμῶν — "and before him we shall persuade/set at rest our hearts." Πείθω (G3982) — to persuade, convince. We argue our own hearts toward confidence in God's presence, using the evidence of love enacted.
20: ὅτι ἐὰν καταγινώσκῃ ἡμῶν ἡ καρδία — "if our heart condemns us." Καταγινώσκω (G2607) — to know against, to judge against; the heart acting as prosecutor against oneself. ὅτι μείζων ἐστὶν ὁ θεὸς τῆς καρδίας ἡμῶν καὶ γινώσκει πάντα — "because God is greater than our heart and knows all things." Two arguments: God is greater than the condemning heart in authority, and his knowledge is total. His omniscience is not a further threat but the ground for appeal: comprehensive divine knowledge will not miss the genuine love enacted, even when conscience cannot assess it rightly.
21: ἐὰν ἡ καρδία μὴ καταγινώσκῃ — "if our heart does not condemn us." The positive corollary of v.20. παρρησίαν ἔχομεν πρὸς τὸν θεόν — "we have boldness toward God." Παρρησία (G3954) — literally "all-speech" — the freedom of unrestricted speech before God. In 1 John it appears at 2:28 (at the parousia), 3:21 (present prayer), and 5:14 (prayer according to his will). The accusative-of-direction πρὸς τὸν θεόν frames prayer as confident approach, not fearful petition.
22: ὃ ἐὰν αἰτῶμεν λαμβάνομεν ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ — "whatever we ask we receive from him." The claim is not unconditional: the ground is given in the ὅτι clause — τὰς ἐντολὰς αὐτοῦ τηροῦμεν καὶ τὰ ἀρεστὰ ἐνώπιον αὐτοῦ ποιοῦμεν — "because we keep his commandments and do what is pleasing in his sight." Ἀρεστά (G701, pleasing things) and commandment-keeping together characterize the relational context in which effective prayer operates. Answered prayer is a feature of abiding relationship, not a technique.
23: αὕτη ἐστὶν ἡ ἐντολὴ αὐτοῦ — "this is his commandment" (singular). The plural commandments of vv.22-24 are summed up as one: ἵνα πιστεύσωμεν τῷ ὀνόματι τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτοῦ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ καὶ ἀγαπῶμεν ἀλλήλους — "that we believe in the name of his Son Jesus Christ and love one another." Two verbs, one command: faith and love as inseparable. Πιστεύειν τῷ ὀνόματι — "to believe in the name" uses the dative of trust — the name carries the person; to trust the name is to trust the one who bears it.
24: ὁ τηρῶν τὰς ἐντολὰς αὐτοῦ ἐν αὐτῷ μένει καὶ αὐτὸς ἐν αὐτῷ — "the one who keeps his commandments abides in him and he in him." The mutual indwelling is stated symmetrically: commandment-keeping → human-in-God abiding → God-in-human abiding. The structure is reciprocal but not equivalent: the human keeps commands; God grants the indwelling. ἐκ τοῦ πνεύματος οὗ ἡμῖν ἔδωκεν — "by the Spirit which he has given us." The Spirit (G4151, capitalized — the Holy Spirit, as in the translation decisions) is the tangible evidence of divine indwelling. The gift is a completed action (ἔδωκεν, aorist) with ongoing implications.
